skill.name: container-grype
skill.description: Guides container vulnerability scanning with Grype, including CI/CD integration, SBOM scanning, risk prioritization, and report generation.
skill.summary: Run and operationalize Grype container vulnerability scans with CI/CD security gates.
skill.author: AgentSecOps

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, filesystem, environment, and script patterns, but most are documentation examples for legitimate vulnerability scanning workflows. The confirmed risks are operational: some CI templates install tools with curl piped to a shell and one Jenkins example mounts the Docker socket, so publication is acceptable only with clear warnings and review guidance.

medium_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped to Shell in CI Templates
medium_findings.description: The CircleCI and Azure examples install Grype by downloading a script from GitHub and piping it directly to a shell. This is a real supply-chain risk if users copy the templates without pinning, checksum verification, or controlled package sources.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is present in executable CI snippets and would execute remote code during builds. Confidence is high, but the context is an example template for a security scanner rather than hidden malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Docker Socket Mounted Into Jenkins Scan Container
medium_findings.description: The Jenkins example mounts /var/run/docker.sock into the Grype container. This is sometimes required for image scanning, but it gives the scan container broad control over the Docker daemon if the container or pipeline is compromised.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Docker socket mount is explicitly present in the Jenkins CI example. It is a legitimate operational pattern, but it materially expands pipeline privileges.

low_findings.title: Educational Security Examples Trigger Static Patterns
low_findings.description: Several findings are false positives from educational examples that demonstrate vulnerable patterns, CVSS concepts, CISA KEV prioritization, or remediation guidance. These examples are not executed by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding files are clearly templates or reference documents, and the flagged lines are used to teach detection and remediation. They should be documented, but they do not show active malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Normal CI Token and Local Configuration Access
low_findings.description: The GitHub token, environment variable, .grype.yaml, cache directory, and report file reads are expected for CI security reporting and Grype configuration. No evidence found that the skill exfiltrates secrets or reads unrelated user files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence appears in CI configuration and local Grype configuration examples. It remains low risk because users may copy the templates into privileged environments.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe to Shell Installation Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The templates include curl piped to sh or bash for tool installation. Users should replace this with pinned releases, package manager installs, or checksum-verified downloads before production use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command pattern is explicit and executable in CI. It is dangerous as a copied template even though it supports a legitimate installation workflow.

dangerous_patterns.title: Privileged Docker Daemon Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The Jenkins template grants the scan container access to the host Docker socket. This can be equivalent to host-level control in many CI environments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The socket mount is visible and semantically meaningful. The risk depends on the CI trust boundary and runner hardening.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Examples Require Input Control
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains many shell command examples for Grype, Docker, Syft, package tools, and report processing. They are expected for a DevSecOps skill, but users should avoid inserting untrusted image names, paths, or pipeline variables without validation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are legitimate examples, but some use CI variables and user-selected targets. Injection risk depends on how downstream users adapt the templates.

content.value_statement: Container images often ship with vulnerable packages that are hard to prioritize. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code run Grype scans, interpret results, and add CI/CD gates.
actual_capabilities: Guides Grype scans for container images, filesystems, and SBOM files.
Explains severity thresholds for CI/CD pipeline security gates.
Helps prioritize findings using CVSS, EPSS, and CISA KEV context.
Provides example configurations for GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins, CircleCI, Azure Pipelines, and Tekton.
Describes output formats including table, JSON, SARIF, and CycloneDX.
Includes remediation guidance for vulnerable packages and base images.
limitations: Requires Grype and any container tooling to be installed in the user environment.
Does not prove exploitability for every reported vulnerability.
CI templates need review before production use, especially remote installers and Docker socket access.
Scan quality depends on current vulnerability databases and accurate package detection.

use_cases.title: Block risky container releases
use_cases.description: Add Grype scans to build pipelines and fail releases when high or critical vulnerabilities are found.

use_cases.title: Prioritize remediation work
use_cases.description: Rank container findings using severity, exploit probability, known exploitation, fix availability, and affected package context.

use_cases.title: Prepare audit evidence
use_cases.description: Generate repeatable scan reports and artifacts that support vulnerability management and software supply chain controls.

prompt_templates.title: Run a first scan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the container-grype skill to scan my image named <image>. Explain the command, expected output, and how to read critical and high findings.

prompt_templates.title: Add a CI gate
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the container-grype skill to design a CI/CD Grype scan for <platform>. Fail on high severity and save a human-readable report as an artifact.

prompt_templates.title: Prioritize a report
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the container-grype skill to help prioritize my Grype results. Focus on CISA KEV, EPSS, CVSS, fix availability, and package ownership.

prompt_templates.title: Design an enterprise workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the container-grype skill to design a container vulnerability management workflow across build, registry, deployment, and scheduled rescans.

output_examples.input: Scan a production image and explain the result.
output_examples.output: A concise scan plan, a severity summary, the highest-risk CVEs, affected packages, recommended updates, and a re-scan step.

output_examples.input: Add Grype to a GitHub Actions pipeline.
output_examples.output: A pipeline outline with checkout, build, scan, SARIF upload, artifact retention, fail threshold, and warnings about template hardening.

output_examples.input: Turn vulnerability results into a remediation plan.
output_examples.output: A prioritized action list grouped by base image updates, application dependency upgrades, accepted risks, owners, and target dates.

best_practices: Pin scanner versions and verify downloads before using CI templates in production.
Treat KEV matches and high EPSS vulnerabilities as urgent even when CVSS is lower.
Store scan reports securely because package inventories can reveal application architecture.
anti_patterns: Do not copy remote installer commands into production CI without verification.
Do not mount the Docker socket into shared runners unless the runner is isolated and trusted.
Do not suppress vulnerabilities without documented reason, owner, and review date.

faq.question: What does this skill help me scan?
faq.answer: It helps scan container images, local filesystems, and SBOM files with Grype.

faq.question: Can it replace a security analyst?
faq.answer: No. It supports triage and workflow design, but a qualified reviewer should approve risk decisions.

faq.question: Does it require Docker access?
faq.answer: Some image scan workflows require Docker or registry access. SBOM-based scans can reduce that requirement.

faq.question: Why use SBOM scanning?
faq.answer: SBOM scanning can be faster, repeatable, and easier to archive for audits.

faq.question: Are the CI templates production ready?
faq.answer: They are examples. Review remote installers, token permissions, runner isolation, and Docker socket access before use.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
